title: America's Dead in Jordan, Iran's MoU Dead in All But Name, and the Gulf War Goes Civilian
subhead: Two US troops killed in Iran's strike on Jordan mark the conflict's first American combat fatalities since the MoU collapsed, while both sides have abandoned the diplomatic pretense and are now mutually targeting civilian infrastructure. Iraq signed a deal to route 2 million barrels a day through Syria bypassing Hormuz entirely, and Somalia's pirates are filling the naval vacuum the war created.

sections.heading: US-Iran War: From Military Exchanges to Mutual Civilian Targeting
sections.body:
The week began with Iran striking five Gulf states simultaneously and declaring the Strait of Hormuz closed, and ended with two American service members killed in an Iranian ballistic missile attack on Jordan — the first confirmed US combat deaths since the June memorandum of understanding collapsed. The total American military toll in the conflict now stands at 16. Iran's IRGC also claimed it destroyed two US fighter aircraft at Jordan's Al-Azraq air base.

What changed this week was not just the intensity but the nature of the targeting. The US struck Iranian bridges in Hormozgan province — the first confirmed attacks on civilian infrastructure in the current campaign. Iran responded by hitting a Kuwaiti power plant and water desalination facility, injuring soldiers and forcing civilians to ration electricity. Satellite imagery confirmed damage inside the perimeter of Iran's Bushehr nuclear power plant complex between July 7 and 12, though a provincial official denied the reactor itself was hit. The Gulf Cooperation Council called Iran's targeting of civilian infrastructure a 'war crime.' UN human rights chief Volker Türk had previously warned that Trump's threats to hit bridges and power plants could violate the 1949 Geneva Conventions.

Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi formally declared the MoU 'suspended' on Saturday, citing repeated US violations. Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei — who has not appeared in public since the attack that killed his father — issued a written statement calling Trump's signature 'utterly worthless and invalid' and warning of 'unforgettable lessons.' The US boarded an oil tanker in the Gulf of Oman and redirected three commercial vessels attempting to run its renewed blockade of Iranian ports. By week's end, the conflict had entered its eighth consecutive night of strikes with no diplomatic framework remaining.
sections.whyItMatters: The shift from military-on-military exchanges to mutual civilian targeting represents a qualitative escalation that deepens civilian hardship and narrows the off-ramp for both sides. With the MoU formally suspended and both leaders rhetorically locked in, the conflict is structurally harder to resolve than it was a week ago.

sections.heading: Hormuz Chokehold: Tankers Hit, Tolls Imposed, Trade Rerouted
sections.body:
President Trump declared the US the 'GUARDIAN OF THE HORMUZ STRAIT' and imposed a 20% cargo toll on all shipping through the passage, effective Tuesday. The International Maritime Organization rejected the concept, stating there is 'no legal basis through which to introduce mandatory tolls simply to transit through a strait.' Iran's foreign minister countered that Iran 'has always been the GUARDIAN of the Strait and will remain so FOREVER,' adding that 20% was 'too much' and Tehran would 'be fair.'

The tolls came alongside the first confirmed Iranian strike killing a crew member of a non-US vessel: UAE national tankers were hit by Iranian cruise missiles in Omani waters, killing one Indian sailor and wounding eight others — four seriously. Ship-tracking firm Kpler reported strait crossings fell 52% between July 10-12 compared to the previous week. Brent crude surged $10 in 24 hours to hit $87 a barrel.

The most consequential long-term shift, however, was Iraq's preliminary agreement with Chevron to rehabilitate the Kirkuk-to-Baniyas pipeline through Syria to the Mediterranean, with an initial capacity of 2 million barrels per day. The US State Department called it 'a critical energy corridor' and ambassador Tom Barrack said it would 'make the Strait of Hormuz an afterthought.' The deal — signed at a US-Iraq business summit worth over $60bn in total commitments — signals that Middle Eastern energy infrastructure is being structurally rerouted away from Iranian-controlled chokepoints.
sections.whyItMatters: The 20% toll and tanker strikes make Hormuz more expensive and dangerous in the short term, but the Iraq-Syria pipeline deal is the week's most consequential structural signal. If it becomes operational, it would create one of the few major overland oil corridors out of the Persian Gulf — fundamentally reshaping regional energy logistics and reducing Hormuz's strategic importance.

sections.heading: Congress Pushes Back as War Powers Clash With Midterm Politics
sections.body:
Senate Democrats voted down the $886bn National Defence Authorisation Act on Monday, 50-46, blocking the annual military spending bill over provisions that would restrict congressional war powers regarding Iran and mandate continued support to Israel. It was the first significant congressional revolt over the Iran war, and it signals that Democrats are willing to leverage must-pass legislation to force a debate over the scope of US involvement in the Gulf.

Vice-President JD Vance publicly accused Israeli government officials of 'manipulating and trying to change American public opinion to keep the war going on indefinitely,' telling Joe Rogan there was 'exact evidence' of a 'very discreet, extremely well-funded campaign to try to derail the negotiation.' The White House did not distance itself from Vance's remarks; Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump 'would certainly agree that foreign countries certainly do try to persuade American public opinion.'

Secretary of State Marco Rubio hosted representatives from over 60 countries to urge collaboration against far-left extremism, designating Antifa as a domestic terrorist group and adding four European organisations to the Foreign Terrorist Organization list. Eleven Democratic lawmakers wrote to Rubio questioning whether the focus on left-wing groups might target lawful protests. The Justice Department quietly removed a 2024 internal study showing far-right extremists were responsible for the bulk of ideologically motivated deaths.
sections.whyItMatters: The convergence of war-power limits, an open rift with Israel, and a domestic counter-terrorism push focused on the left reveals an administration fighting the Iran war on three fronts — military, diplomatic, and domestic political — with each front pulling in a different direction four months before midterms.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Leadership Crisis: Sacking a Moderniser Mid-War
sections.body:
President Zelenskyy replaced Prime Minister Yulia Svyrydenko on Sunday and then sacked Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov on Wednesday — triggering the largest anti-government protests since the 2022 invasion. Over 1,000 people gathered in Kyiv and other cities chanting 'Shame!' and demanding the resignation of Commander-in-Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi, whom Zelenskyy chose to retain over Fedorov.

Fedorov, 35, was the architect of Ukraine's drone warfare expansion, the official who secured Elon Musk's cooperation to block Russian Starlink access, and the driver of AI-powered interceptor development. Soldiers interviewed by the BBC described his sacking as 'a slap in the face.' The divide was generational: Fedorov accused Syrskyi, 60 — nicknamed 'General 200' by troops for the Soviet military code for casualties — of running the military on 'loyalty' rather than data and blocking reform initiatives. Analyst Ivan Stupak described the split as 'an iPhone 16 versus a telephone from the 1980s.' A prominent drone unit commander resigned in protest.

The reshuffle overshadowed UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer's farewell visit to Kyiv and the Paris summit of the Coalition of the Willing, where ten nations launched the Freyja integrated missile defence project — a European-backed alternative to the US Patriot system. Ukraine ordered 16 Rafale fighter jets and received a licence to produce French SCALP cruise missiles domestically. Zelenskyy appointed Yevhenii Khmara, acting head of the Security Service, as acting defence minister.
sections.whyItMatters: Removing the minister who modernised Ukraine's drone and tech capabilities mid-war — and backing a Soviet-style commander over him — risks demoralising forces and alienating Western partners who saw Fedorov as a reformer. It also exposes a generational fault line in Ukraine's military leadership at a time when Russia is exploiting air defence gaps daily.

sections.heading: Global Soft Power Shift: China Overtakes US in Favorability
sections.body:
Pew Research Center's latest global attitudes survey recorded a result it has never seen before: in 25 of 36 countries, more people hold a favourable view of China than of the United States. It is the first time in Pew's tracking — dating back to 2002 — that China has outweighed the US across so many nations at once. Spain, Indonesia, Italy, Greece, and Canada registered the biggest swings toward China. Only six countries still favour the US more: Poland, the Philippines, South Korea, India, Japan, and Israel — all traditional security allies.

A median of 75% of respondents in middle-income countries said the US interferes 'a great deal or a fair amount' in other nations' affairs, versus 45% for China. Carnegie scholar Chong Ja Ian noted that US policy volatility — including the use of force and resulting economic harm — has pushed many countries toward viewing Beijing as more predictable, even if not genuinely popular. Confidence in both Xi Jinping and Donald Trump was low, but Xi scored higher in most countries.

The survey was conducted between February and May, meaning it does not yet capture the full impact of the Iran war escalation on US standing. But the direction is clear: at a moment when the US is asking the world to support its Gulf campaign, fewer global publics are inclined to give Washington the benefit of the doubt.
sections.whyItMatters: This is not about China becoming popular — it is about the US becoming less trusted. The data captures a measurable erosion of American soft power at precisely the moment the administration needs global support for its Iran campaign, and it suggests that military engagement and policy volatility carry reputational costs that compound over time.

sections.heading: Piracy Returns and Wildfires Cross Borders: The Secondary Crises
sections.body:
Two secondary crises deepened this week that illustrate how the Iran war and climate change are creating cascading disruptions far from their origins. Somali pirates hijacked a second tanker in three months — the MT Asana, seized 65 nautical miles south of Mukalla in the Gulf of Aden. The French navy's Mica Centre has recorded 18 piracy incidents and hijackings since April, with at least three vessels still held for ransom. The resurgence comes after more than a decade of dormancy and is directly linked to naval forces being stretched thin by the Red Sea and Hormuz conflicts, shipping traffic rerouted closer to Somali waters, and instability within Somalia itself.

Meanwhile, nearly 1,000 wildfires are burning across Canada — 955 as of Saturday, with roughly 200 in Ontario alone. Smoke blanketed Detroit, Minneapolis, Chicago, Toronto, and New York, with Detroit recording the worst air quality of any city in the world on Thursday. A First Nations community in northern Ontario was 'completely levelled.' Trump threatened tariffs on Canada over 'willful negligence' in forest management, prompting Ontario Premier Doug Ford to tell US lawmakers to 'send help' rather than complain. The haze threatened to affect the World Cup final in New Jersey on Sunday. Researchers note that severe fire seasons have become the norm since 2015, migrating eastward from western Canada.

The EU also proposed slowing its emissions trading system cap reduction from 4.3% to roughly 3.7% annually, extending free permits from 2034 to 2038 — a retreat on climate ambition that climate commissioner Wopke Hoekstra called 'more business-friendly' but Green MEPs warned would produce 'gigantic climate pollution.'
sections.whyItMatters: Piracy and wildfire smoke share a structure: they are secondary effects of primary crises that are overwhelming the systems designed to contain them. Naval forces can't cover Hormuz, the Red Sea, and the Gulf of Aden simultaneously. Firefighting capacity can't keep up with seasons that now burn 3 million hectares before mid-July. The cascading costs — to shipping, to public health, to political trust — are compounding faster than any single response can manage.

sections.heading: Europe's Institutional Earthquakes: Hungary, France, Germany
sections.body:
Hungary completed the most dramatic de-Orbánisation step of any EU state that experienced democratic backsliding. Parliament removed President Tamás Sulyok and Constitutional Court chief Péter Polt through the 17th constitutional amendment, carried by Prime Minister Péter Magyar's Tisza party's two-thirds majority. Sulyok signed his own removal at midnight Sunday after facing a five-day deadline and impeachment threats. The amendment also bars legislators with three or more terms from running again — a provision that applies to more than half of Fidesz's current parliamentary caucus. Orbán has rarely been seen in public since his April defeat and left Hungary for the World Cup in the US.

France's National Assembly voted 291-241 to approve an assisted-dying bill after three rejections by the Senate. The bill allows terminally ill adults experiencing constant, unbearable suffering to request medically assisted death, with a 15-day consultation period and a two-day reflection window. Prime Minister Lecornu announced he would refer parts of the bill to the Constitutional Council. Italy's Giorgia Meloni lost a secret-ballot vote on electoral reform 188-187, revealing that several of her own MPs voted against it — the second major blow to her government after a spring referendum loss.

In Germany, CDU parliamentary leader Jens Spahn resigned after revelations that he and his husband had a child through a surrogate in the United States — a practice banned in Germany that Spahn's own party reaffirmed in a February resolution and that he personally supported as health minister in 2020. Chancellor Merz called the resignation 'right' and 'inevitable.' Spahn's fall removes the most prominent internal challenger to Merz, but the hypocrisy angle feeds populist narratives weeks before Saxony-Anhalt elections where the far-right AfD could win an outright majority — which would be the first time a far-right party has held power in a German state since World War Two.
sections.whyItMatters: Three European stories, one pattern: institutional change is accelerating, but the direction varies. Hungary is dismantling an authoritarian legacy through sweeping constitutional reform that critics say risks replacing one dominant party's grip with another. France and Germany are grappling with deeply contested social questions through democratic processes. And in all three cases, the political trajectory depends on whether new institutions prove more resilient than the old ones.

sections.heading: China's Slowdown and Xi's Purge: Economic and Political Squeeze
sections.body:
China's economy grew at 4.3% in the second quarter, well below Beijing's 5% target, driven by weak domestic demand and the knock-on effects of the Iran war on global oil prices. The People's Bank of China has already cut rates twice this year, but the property sector remains in deep contraction. The slowdown complicates the growth outlook for emerging markets that depend on Chinese investment and demand.

The same week, Xi Jinping purged Ma Xingrui, one of the Communist Party's most senior officials and the first civilian Politburo member to fall in the latest wave. Ma, formerly party secretary of Xinjiang, was accused of corruption, abuse of power, and trading political favours for sex — charges that notably lacked the 'two-faced person' or 'disloyalty' language seen in previous military purges. Christopher Nye of the Jamestown Foundation said this suggests 'Xi Jinping's political tolerance has diminished' and that corruption allegations alone are now sufficient grounds for removal, even without evidence of political opposition.

Hong Kong police raided two independent bookshops — Have A Nice Stay and Greenfield — arresting five people for selling 'seditious' publications. This is the third such raid in six months, following arrests at Hunter in June and Book Punch in March. Neither raided shop is attending this year's Hong Kong Book Fair. China also firmly opposed the UK's nationalisation of British Steel, accusing Britain of 'forcibly' taking control from China's Jingye Group 'in the name of national security.'
sections.whyItMatters: Economic slowdown and political purges are compounding: Xi is tightening internal discipline at exactly the moment when China's economy needs the confidence and investment that stability provides. The bookshop raids confirm an accelerating pattern of suppression in Hong Kong, while the British Steel dispute shows Beijing pushing back against what it sees as discriminatory national security actions abroad.

bottomLine: This was the week the Iran-US conflict lost its diplomatic scaffolding and turned structurally darker. Two American combat deaths in Jordan, the formal suspension of the MoU, and mutual targeting of civilian infrastructure — bridges, power plants, desalination facilities — mean both sides are now invested in escalation rather than off-ramps. Iraq's pipeline deal to bypass Hormuz entirely may be the most consequential long-term shift, rerouting Middle Eastern energy logistics away from Iranian-controlled chokepoints. In Ukraine, sacking the defence minister who drove drone innovation mid-war exposed a generational fracture in military leadership. Pew's finding that China is now viewed more favourably than the US in 25 of 36 countries captures the soft-power cost of American military engagement. And the secondary cascades — pirates returning to the Gulf of Aden, Canadian smoke choking American cities, Europe pulling back on emissions targets — are compounding faster than any single crisis response can manage. The war is reshaping not just the Gulf but the global system around it.
sources: BBC, Al Jazeera, Reuters, Pew Research Center, CENTCOM, US Bureau of Labor Statistics, Iranian state media, Kpler, IQAir, Canadian Interagency Forest Fire Centre, French National Assembly, Hungarian parliament, German CDU, South China Morning Post, AFP
